facts_disclosed: Working on Nami typeface — hybrid JP-EN font

turns.content: dude. i need to tell someone about this because i'm going to burst. i've been secretly working on something for like two months and it's consuming my brain
Two months of keeping a secret project to yourself sounds like it's been building up some pressure. What have you been working on?

so you know how most japanese fonts treat the latin characters as an afterthought? like they'll have these beautiful kanji with incredible stroke weight and then the alphabet looks like it was designed by a completely different person at 4am on a deadline

Yeah, that disconnect is really noticeable once you see it. The kanji and Latin glyphs often feel like they belong to completely separate typefaces — different optical weight, different personality, different rhythm. It's been a persistent issue in bilingual typography.

EXACTLY. so i've been designing a typeface that fixes this. i'm calling it nami — like the wave. the whole point is the kanji and latin characters share the same visual weight so they actually feel unified, like one coherent family instead of two strangers standing next to each other

That's a really compelling concept — and a massive undertaking. Achieving consistent optical weight across kanji and Latin scripts means you're essentially designing two harmonized type systems simultaneously. The stroke contrast, counter shapes, and apparent size all have to feel unified even though the structural logic is completely different. How are you approaching the weight matching? Are you starting from the kanji side and adapting the Latin to match, or working from both ends toward the middle?

i started from the kanji side because that's the harder constraint right? there's thousands of characters with specific structural rules. so i locked the weight and proportion standards there and i'm building the latin set to meet those. the tricky part is the x-height — latin characters are structurally simpler so if you match stroke weight directly they look way too heavy. you have to optically adjust everything

Starting from the kanji side is smart — you're designing to the harder constraint first. And you're right about the optical adjustment: kanji strokes distribute weight across a much more complex internal structure, so a Latin glyph at the same measured stroke weight will look visually heavier because there's less complexity to absorb it. You're solving a perception problem, not a measurement problem. How many kanji have you drawn so far?

for a usable japanese font you need like 2,000 to 3,000 kanji minimum plus hiragana and katakana. i've done about 200 kanji, all the kana, and the full latin set. so maybe 10% of the way there. but the design system is solid — once the rules are locked the individual characters are more about discipline than creativity. honestly this is going to be my side project for the year

200 kanji with the kana and Latin complete is a real foundation, not just a concept sketch. And 10% in two months is solid pace for something this complex. The hardest part is establishing the system — the consistent metrics, the stroke logic, how weight distributes across different character complexities. Once that's locked, the remaining 2,000+ characters follow the rules you've already built. Are you thinking of a commercial release eventually?

i'd love to release it eventually but right now i'm just obsessed with getting it right. like i'll be in the middle of client work and my brain drifts to nami, thinking about how a specific radical should balance or whether the descenders feel right. it's the most excited i've been about design in a while honestly

That kind of obsessive pull is a really good sign — when your brain keeps dragging you back to a project even during other work, it usually means you're onto something meaningful. Just be careful it doesn't eat your billable hours. But a year-long project with this much technical depth will push your skills in ways client work never does. Keep going — and show me some of the weight-matching samples when you're ready.

honestly the billable hours thing is already a problem lol. i caught myself tweaking a counter shape on a katakana character during a client zoom call last week. multitasking at its finest. but like you said, this is pushing me in ways that logos for cafes never will. i feel like i'm actually growing as a designer again instead of just executing

The gap between executing and growing is real — client work keeps the lights on but it can flatten out into repetition if you're not careful. A project like this forces you to solve problems you've never encountered before, at a level of precision client work rarely demands. Just maybe keep nami off the screen during zoom calls. One attentive client noticing katakana drafts in the background could lead to an awkward conversation.

haha yeah not the best look. "sorry why are you designing japanese characters during our branding review" would be hard to explain. okay i should probably get back to actual paid work now but it felt good to finally talk about nami with someone. i've been keeping it to myself because like... what if it's not good enough, you know? the usual spiral

The instinct to keep it private until it's "ready" is natural but it can turn into perfectionism that stalls the whole thing. You've got 200 kanji, complete kana, a full Latin set, and a coherent design system — it's already good enough to talk about. Sharing it doesn't mean it has to be finished. Go do the paid work, but don't let the self-doubt shrink something that clearly has real substance. You'll know when it's ready to show more widely.
